---
title: "4.2.1 High-level languages and low-level languages"
subtitle: "Software P165-P167"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.74em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)

## Programming Languages

### Programs tell computers what to do

Programmers use programming languages to communicate with computers.

Computers only understand their own language, called <span class="term">machine code</span> (机器码).

A program needs to be translated into machine code before it can be understood by a computer.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.14 A Scratch multiplication table test program screenshot placeholder
:::

## Computer Programs

### Specific tasks

A <span class="term">computer program</span> is a list of instructions that enable a computer to perform a specific task.

Computer programs can be written in:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">high-level languages</span>
- <span class="term">low-level languages</span>
:::

Most programmers write programs in high-level languages.

## High-Level Languages

### Problem-oriented programming

<span class="term">High-level languages</span> enable a programmer to focus on the problem to be solved.

They require no knowledge of:

::: {.tight-list}
- the hardware of the computer that will use the program
- the instruction set of the computer
:::

Many high-level programming languages are portable and can be used on different types of computer.

## High-Level Languages

### Easier for programmers

High-level languages are designed with programmers in mind.

Programs written in a high-level language are easier to:

::: {.tight-list}
- read and understand because the language is closer to English
- write in a shorter time
- debug at the development stage
- maintain once in use
:::

## High-Level Language Example

### Adding two numbers

The textbook gives this example of a single high-level program statement:

```text
Sum := FirstNumber + SecondNumber
```

It shows how easy it is to understand what is happening in a high-level language program.

Examples of high-level languages include C++, Delphi, Java, Pascal, Python and Visual Basic.

## Low-Level Languages

### Hardware-specific languages

<span class="term">Low-level languages</span> relate to the specific architecture and hardware of a particular type of computer.

Low-level languages can refer to:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">machine code</span>, the binary instructions that a computer understands
- <span class="term">assembly language</span>, which needs to be translated into machine code
:::

## Machine Code

### Binary instructions

Programmers do not usually write in machine code because it is difficult to understand.

It can also be complicated to manage:

::: {.tight-list}
- data manipulation
- data storage
:::

Machine code is usually shown in hexadecimal.

## Table 4.2

### High-level language comparison

::: {.clean-table}
| High-level advantages | High-level disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Independent of the type of computer being used | Programs can be larger |
| Easier to read, write and understand programs | Programs can take longer to execute |
| Quicker to write programs | Programs may not make use of special hardware |
| Easier and quicker to debug |  |
| Easier to maintain programs in use |  |
:::

## Table 4.2

### Low-level language comparison

::: {.clean-table}
| Low-level advantages | Low-level disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Can make use of special hardware | It takes longer to write and debug programs |
| Includes special machine-dependent instructions | Programs are more difficult to understand |
| Can write code that does not take up much space in primary memory |  |
| Can write code that performs a task very quickly |  |
:::

## Classroom Check

### Compare high-level and low-level languages

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- computers only understand <span class="term">machine code</span>
- high-level languages are closer to English and easier to read, write, debug and maintain
- high-level languages are often portable
- low-level languages depend on architecture and hardware
- low-level languages include machine code and assembly language
- low-level code can use special hardware and run quickly, but is harder to write and debug
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Assembly languages

The next deck explains assembly language and mnemonics.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)
